full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I preached my first ever sermon on Trinity Sunday.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> It was 1985, and I was graduating from high school.<|13.00|> <|13.00|> My youth group selected me to deliver the sermon and represent the graduates on Youth Sunday.<|21.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You see, many Episcopal churches hold their special service for youth and graduates on<|8.20|> <|8.20|> Trinity Sunday.<|9.14|> <|9.14|> My second time to preach on Trinity Sunday was when I served as a seminary intern at<|18.54|> <|18.54|> St. Mary Magdalene in Boulder.<|20.30|> <|21.38|> Are you starting to detect a pattern here?<|24.10|> <|24.10|> No.<|25.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It seems that preaching on this day is like drawing the short straw.<|4.84|> <|6.16|> It seems that no priest really wants to have to tackle the Trinity.<|11.56|> <|12.70|> And that's what I thought too when I saw that I was preaching today.<|16.88|> <|17.32|> I've managed to avoid it the whole time I've been here.<|19.94|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And hopefully, hopefully for your sake and mine, I've learned a thing or two that informs<|8.90|> <|8.90|> my theology and my preaching since the last two times I stood in a pulpit on this day.<|15.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But let's be honest, Trinity Sunday is a bit of a hard sell, isn't it?<|7.86|> <|8.52|> While most of our festivals and feast days honor dramatic events,<|14.32|> <|15.24|> the birth of Jesus, the resurrection, the coming of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost,<|23.64|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Trinity Sunday asks us to celebrate an idea, a theological abstraction that seems more at home in the seminary classroom than in a parish.<|16.32|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And even worse, it asks us to celebrate an idea that we can't wrap our heads around,<|7.68|> <|8.38|> no matter how hard we try.<|10.24|> <|11.12|> And scripture tells us that we are doomed to fail if we try.<|15.34|> <|16.48|> We read in Psalms, great is the Lord and greatly to be praised,<|21.16|> <|21.76|> and his greatness is unsearchable.<|25.04|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.00|> Such knowledge is too wonderful for me.<|4.04|> <|4.54|> It is high.<|5.40|> <|6.34|> I cannot attain it.<|7.96|> <|8.48|> You're getting the message, right?<|9.74|> <|10.94|> And from Isaiah, God says,<|13.66|> <|13.66|> For my thoughts are not your thoughts,<|16.52|> <|17.14|> neither are your ways my ways, declares the Lord.<|21.56|> <|22.54|> For as the heavens are higher than the earth,<|24.68|> <|24.92|> so are my ways higher than yours.<|27.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and my thoughts than your thoughts.<|2.90|> <|4.40|> And the Apostle Paul tells us that the Spirit searches everything,<|9.18|> <|9.48|> even the depths of God,<|11.16|> <|11.60|> and that no one comprehends the things of God<|15.32|> <|15.32|> except the Spirit of God.<|18.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So what are we doing on this day?<|4.00|> <|4.00|> Doesn't it feel a little hopeless?<|7.00|> <|7.00|> Because the Trinity is, in fact, and I'm not being glib,<|12.00|> <|12.00|> the Trinity is a mystery,<|15.00|> <|15.00|> one that Episcopalians might be more comfortable with<|18.00|> <|18.00|> than some other denominations.<|21.00|> <|21.00|> We love gray.<|23.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Richard has often said Episcopalians have a high tolerance for ambiguity.<|6.10|> <|6.92|> Welcome to Trinity Sunday.<|8.48|> <|10.00|> He doesn't say the welcome to Trinity Sunday part.<|12.72|> <|15.18|> But we trust it to be true.<|18.78|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There is one God.<|2.26|> <|3.76|> God is revealed in Jesus, who is God.<|8.20|> <|9.10|> And God is revealed in the Spirit, who is also God.<|15.30|> <|16.58|> And to steal a line from Winston Churchill,<|19.10|> <|19.60|> it is a riddle wrapped in a mystery inside an enigma.<|24.38|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.52|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And so, my friends, this is actually permission to just let go.<|6.92|> <|8.00|> Let go of our attempts to create a plausible analogy,<|13.04|> <|13.76|> many of which you have probably heard from a pulpit.<|17.20|> <|18.14|> People who say that the Trinity is like a shamrock,<|21.14|> <|21.70|> or it's like an egg, or it's like water.<|25.42|> <|25.42|> Water.<|25.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.00|> Gas, solid, liquid, all at the same time.<|4.10|> <|5.12|> No, it's not.<|6.04|> <|8.62|> All analogies fall short.<|12.16|> <|13.68|> We can only trust, as Jesus said in today's text from John,<|20.10|> <|21.22|> that the truth will be revealed to us when we can bear it.<|26.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> it. And so the question has been raised, and I think it is the right question for us.<|7.36|> <|8.42|> What if we let go, we step out of the classroom, and we don't look at this day as being about<|17.56|> <|17.56|> doctrine, but rather we look at Trinity Sunday about God's desire to be known?<|27.18|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Our response to the Trinity then could be delight instead of consternation and confusion.<|8.88|> <|10.20|> An opportunity to know God.<|14.76|> <|15.68|> Because my friends, being known and being understood are not the same thing.<|21.84|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The God who is drawn close to us in Jesus is the same God who spoke everything into existence.<|9.46|> <|10.48|> That's what the Holy Spirit shows us and what Jesus shows us.<|17.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The Trinity makes God visible.<|4.06|> <|4.98|> God wants to be known, and the Trinity is God's way of being known.<|11.18|> <|12.18|> We see signs throughout Scripture.<|15.26|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> forth from rocks<|6.84|> <|6.84|> and angels do not come to lunch,<|10.64|> <|11.44|> which is to say most days.<|14.26|> <|15.48|> We experience Trinity and know God through the ordinary.<|23.06|>
